product: Visceral (Research Engine - A better way to survey)

system_description.overview: Natural Language Labs Inc. provides Visceral, an AI-automated Market Research platform that allows users to enter a high-level business objective, against which the system launches agentic and human-assisted tools to source new, proprietary, first-party data. The platform integrates AI-assisted surveys, generative R&D tools, media monitoring, and expert support.

products.AI-Assisted Surveys: Prompt-based survey creation, automated logic validation, sample company integrations, automated analytics
products.Generative R&D: In-market conjoint that tests concepts by launching ads and AI-generated websites and measuring response
products.Other Research Tools: Media Monitoring, Competitive Monitoring, Secondary Research
products.Integrated Expert Support: In-app access to research experts, ongoing software support

infrastructure.cloud_provider: AWS (carve-out method — AWS data center controls excluded from scope)
regions: Not explicitly named — multiple AWS availability zones mentioned
infrastructure.physical_presence: 480 Main St. New York, NY 10044 (no physical servers; all data processed on cloud services)
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (explicitly: 'high availability data centers with multiple availability zones'; DR plan mentions spinning up multiple servers across all availability zones in AWS)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: System hosted in AWS VPC; segmented VPCs and ACLs (access control lists) in use; multiple named VPCs visible in network diagram: visceral-2025-vpc, visceral-external-data, chroma_private_vpc, visceral-main-vpc; network segmentation by sensitivity and criticality (CC6.7)
network_architecture.firewalls: Network firewalls implemented, configured and managed; firewall rules reviewed annually; firewall access restricted to authorized personnel; system firewalls on application gateway and production network; IDS/IPS via AWS
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via AWS ('Natural Language Labs Inc. employs IDS and IPS via AWS to identify and protect against threats in conjunction with its security policy network settings')
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access done through a bastion host with a VPN
network_architecture.tls: TLS with certificates from an established third-party certificate authority
network_architecture.content_filtering: All systems restricted to internet via content filtering system routed through proxy server
network_architecture.topology: Internet traffic enters through segmented VPCs; multiple VPC clusters (server-cluster, workload-cluster, firewall-cluster, chroma private, main-vpc); bastion host for remote access; IDS/IPS at network boundary; anti-virus on all servers and endpoints

application_architecture.pattern: Microservices (multiple named clusters visible in network diagram: server-cluster, workload-cluster, firewall-cluster; separate VPCs for different workloads)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments (CC8.1.4: 'Changes to application and system infrastructure are developed and tested in a separate development or test environment before implementation')

databases: Production database mentioned (access restricted per CC5.2.3, CC6.1.6); specific DB technology not named
data_storage.backup_frequency: Daily (CC7.4.2: 'Backups are taken daily, multi-availability zones are configured')

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: AWS IAM (password policy references AWS; 'cloud provider key management service')
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for all remote access and for user accounts with administrative access to production platform; MFA required for source code management tool access; MFA for employee email
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day of termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: AWS password policy: periodic forced changes, password expiry, complexity requirements; accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts; no local password rule modification by users; guest/anonymous logins not allowed

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider KMS (CC6.1.5: 'The organization uses its cloud provider key management service to encrypt data at rest')
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS (cloud provider key management service; access to keys restricted to authorized individuals)

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Not named (source code management tool referenced with MFA and branch protection)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request to production (CC8.1.2); developers cannot push to production without approval
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline configurations and rollback capability (CC7.1.1: 'Baseline configurations are retained within the infrastructure as a code and a configuration management tool is used'); specific tool not named
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management set up by CTO/Management; monthly patch management reviews via ITLC meetings; workstations on automatic updates; daily compliance scans

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Monitoring tool (unnamed) — continuously monitors infrastructure performance; alerts on predefined thresholds
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled at application and infrastructure level (administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions, security configurations, permissions, roles); access to log configuration restricted
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts to IT management for high/critical risk events; incident management/ticketing system used

third_party_services.purpose: Cloud infrastructure, data center services, VPC, IDS/IPS, KMS
Survey respondent sourcing with routing software for demographic traits

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and malware protection installed on all production servers (continuous auto-scan; virus definitions updated weekly) and on Mac workstations/laptops; e-mail scanning included
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications (CC7.1.2); self-assessment scan prior to every production release or annually; internal IT infrastructure team performs scans
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party (CC4.1)

untestable: CC2.2.3 — No significant personnel changes made during engagement period
CC3.2.1 — Cybersecurity insurance: no cyber incidents reported during engagement period
CC6.5.2 — Customer data deletion: no customer terminations during engagement period
CC7.3.1 — Security incident response: no security incidents reported during engagement period
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical access to data center facilities: AWS is responsible for physical security
A1.2 — Environmental protections: Visceral has no office/physical servers; managed by building management/AWS
notable_controls: Risk and Governance Executive Committee (RGEC) with formal charter meeting quarterly to review internal controls
IT Leadership Committee (ITLC) meeting weekly to review security operations and key performance metrics
Quarterly user access reviews including administrative accounts
Monthly automated vulnerability scans with automated patch management
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
BCDR tested annually across different loss scenarios with multi-AZ failover

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-AZ AWS deployment with multiple VPCs, IaC-referenced configuration management, daily backups. No multi-region failover and no CDN mentioned. Cloud regions not explicitly named.

application_architecture.rationale: Multiple VPC clusters suggest microservices orientation; separate dev/prod environments; branch protection; code review required. However, specific languages, frameworks, and CI/CD tools are not disclosed.

data_layer.rationale: Daily backups, multi-AZ, data classification and retention policies, per-tenant segregation, AWS KMS at-rest encryption. No specific DB technologies, caching, or data warehouse disclosed.

security_posture.rationale: Strong posture: IDS/IPS, annual external pen test, monthly automated vuln scans, antivirus on all endpoints and servers, firewalls, MFA for remote access and admin, cybersecurity insurance, quarterly access reviews, log protection, content filtering. No WAF named specifically.

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection enforced, IaC referenced, separate environments, automated patch management, monthly patch reviews. CI/CD tool and source control platform not named; container orchestration not disclosed.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy exists, annually reviewed and tested across loss scenarios, daily backups, multi-AZ failover, cybersecurity insurance. No multi-region failover, no RTO/RPO disclosed.

vendor_diversity.rationale: AWS is the dominant infrastructure provider with no secondary cloud. Sample providers mentioned but unnamed. Very limited vendor diversity disclosed — heavy AWS dependency.

overall.rationale: Solid SOC 2 Type 2 with zero exceptions across 57 controls. Strong governance (RGEC, ITLC), good security tooling (annual pen test, monthly scans, IDS/IPS, MFA). Main gaps: single cloud provider, no CDN, no multi-region, key tech stack (language, CI/CD, DB) undisclosed.

red_flags: Single cloud provider (AWS only) with no secondary provider or multi-region failover
RTO and RPO not disclosed — recovery time objectives unknown
Specific CI/CD tools, source control platform, and programming languages not named — tech transparency is low
yellow_flags: No WAF explicitly named despite internet-facing survey platform
No CDN mentioned for a web-based platform with global survey respondent base
Container orchestration not disclosed — unclear if containerized workloads have security controls
SIEM tool not named despite logging controls being in place
Sample/respondent providers not named — third-party concentration risk unclear
green_flags: Clean SOC 2 Type 2 audit with zero exceptions across 57 controls tested
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
Multi-AZ deployment with daily backups and multi-AZ failover
Quarterly user access reviews including admin accounts
MFA enforced across remote access, admin production access, and source code management
Formal RGEC and ITLC governance committees with regular meeting cadences
BCDR tested annually across multiple loss scenarios
Formal Risk and Governance Executive Committee with semiannual internal controls review
key_observations: The company branded as 'Visceral' in the report but legal entity is Natural Language Labs Inc. — worth noting for diligence tracking

Network diagram (p.18) reveals multiple named VPCs (visceral-2025-vpc, visceral-external-data, chroma_private_vpc, visceral-main-vpc) suggesting a complex multi-cluster AWS architecture with separate data-processing workloads
The 'chroma_private_vpc' naming suggests use of Chroma vector database for AI/embedding workloads — interesting for an AI market research platform
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria included (full 5-criteria scope) — higher bar than security-only reports
ITLC meets weekly which is unusually frequent for a company of this stage — suggests engineering-led security culture
Product integrates with 'major sample providers' with routing software — third-party data pipeline risk not fully characterized in SOC controls
